speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Before anyone writes code or worries about commands, we need a map of the tools. This lesson is a guided tour of five different ways to work with GitHub: the website, a browser-based editor, a desktop editor, a desktop Git app, and the command line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the big idea is not, pick one forever. It's, learn the landscape so later instructions make sense.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And that matters because there is no single right way to use GitHub. Some people prefer the browser. Some want a visual desktop app. Some love a terminal. Many people switch tools depending on the task.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels especially important for accessibility. If one path works better with your screen reader or your low vision setup, that path is valid.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In fact, this workshop is designed so exercises can be completed using any of the five paths. When later lessons say something like, open the file, create a branch, or make a commit, the meaning stays the same even if the interface changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there was an important note for screen reader users too, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The tools are described by how they behave, not by visual appearance. If a path works well with a screen reader, we say so directly. That's the frame for this whole lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's get the quick overview in place. Path one is GitHub dot com in your browser. No installation. Best for issues, pull requests, repository navigation, and code review. Path two is github dot dev, also in the browser. It feels like VS Code running in a tab. No installation. Best for editing files, making changes across multiple files, and quick commits. Path three is VS Code desktop. That does require installation. It's best for full development: editing, extensions, a terminal, debugging, and tools like GitHub Copilot. Path four is GitHub Desktop. Also installed locally. It's a visual Git client, good for branching, committing, and syncing changes without typing commands. Path five is GitHub CLI, the command-line tool called G H. It also requires installation and is best for scripting, automation, and learners who prefer the terminal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And learners do not need all five.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not even close. A very common pattern is: start with GitHub dot com on day one, then add VS Code on day two. The other paths are available if they fit your workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the simplest entry point: GitHub dot com in the browser.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the main website. If I can sign in with a browser, I can use it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Every repository, issue, pull request, and setting lives there. No installation is required. What can you do on GitHub dot com? You can browse repositories, files, and folders. You can create, comment on, and close issues. You can open, review, and merge pull requests. You can edit individual files using the built-in web editor. You can manage labels, milestones, and project boards. And you can configure repository settings, including things like branch protection.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it handles a lot of collaboration and repository management.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. But there are limits. You cannot run code or tests locally there. You do not get a full-featured code editor with local extensions. And you cannot make offline changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's slow down for accessibility. What is the screen reader experience like?

speaker: ALEX
text: Strong overall. GitHub dot com has solid screen reader support. Pages use ARIA landmarks. Headings are fairly consistent. Keyboard shortcuts are available for many actions. A few navigation patterns matter a lot. On many pages, pressing S can jump to the main content or search field depending on context, and slash can open search. In browse mode, D moves between landmarks. H moves between headings. In a repository, the tabs like Code, Issues, and Pull Requests sit inside the repository navigation landmark. There's also a shortcut sequence, G then I, that jumps directly to Issues from within a repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the kind of thing that turns GitHub from overwhelming into navigable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. And if you're new to GitHub with a screen reader, there are later lessons that go deeper into page structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How about low vision users?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub has several useful appearance options. You can choose light, dark, light high contrast, dark high contrast, or sync with your operating system. It also generally adapts well to browser zoom, including high zoom levels, often up to four hundred percent without horizontal scrolling on most pages. There are also practical tips. High contrast themes can make borders and text much easier to distinguish. At higher zoom levels, some layout changes happen, like sidebars collapsing into menus. And enabling link underlines can help if color alone is not enough to identify links.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm deciding whether GitHub dot com is a reasonable starting place, the answer is yes for many learners, especially for reading, navigating, reviewing, and making small edits.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And that last point matters: for a single-file edit, GitHub dot com is often the simplest tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's move to github dot dev.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This one is easy to confuse with GitHub dot com. What's the difference?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of github dot dev as a richer editor experience in the browser. It looks and behaves a lot like VS Code, but it runs entirely in a tab. You stay signed in, you don't install anything, and you get a file explorer, tabs, diff views, staging, and commits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it fills the gap between simple web edits and a full local editor.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great way to say it. In github dot dev, you can edit multiple files in a VS Code-like interface. You can view diffs, stage changes, commit directly to a branch, and use many browser-compatible VS Code extensions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the limits?

speaker: ALEX
text: You cannot truly run code, build projects, or execute terminal commands the way you can locally. Some terminal or preview elements may exist, but not as a full local runtime for Git operations and development workflows. You also cannot use extensions that depend on a local runtime, like many debuggers or compiled tools. And, like any browser-based tool, you cannot work offline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you open it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three easy ways. From a repository page, press the period key. Or change github dot com to github dot dev in the URL bar. Or, while viewing a file on GitHub dot com, press period to open that repository in the editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And accessibility?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because github dot dev is basically VS Code in the browser, much of the same screen reader support and keyboard navigation applies. The command palette is available with Control Shift P on Windows or Command Shift P on a Mac. Editor keybindings generally work. One practical note: some screen readers may need focus mode or application mode to interact smoothly with the editor area. If keys don't seem to reach the editor, try Escape to settle focus, then open the command palette.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When would you choose github dot dev over the regular GitHub website?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use github dot dev when you need to edit more than one file in a single commit, or when you want a coding-editor experience without installing software. If you only need to fix one file, the plain GitHub dot com edit flow is often simpler.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now the local tools begin, starting with VS Code desktop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the big one for many developers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Visual Studio Code is a free desktop editor from Microsoft. It has built-in Git support, an integrated terminal, extensive extension support, and GitHub integration. Installation is required, and setup is covered in the workshop's pre-work material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What can learners do once it's installed?

speaker: ALEX
text: A lot. You can edit files with syntax highlighting and IntelliSense. You can use the integrated terminal to run Git commands, scripts, and programs. You can stage, commit, push, and pull using either the Source Control panel or the terminal. You can run and debug code. You can use GitHub Copilot for code suggestions, chat, and review features. And you can work offline, with syncing happening once you reconnect.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like the full development environment.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. But there's an important limitation in the source material too: some tasks are not available by default without extensions or extra setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Such as?

speaker: ALEX
text: Depending on what you want to do, things like specialized GitHub workflows, advanced review experiences, language tooling, accessibility helpers, or debugging support may require installing extensions. So VS Code is powerful out of the box, but not every feature appears automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How is the screen reader experience?

speaker: ALEX
text: VS Code has substantial accessibility support, but it expects a bit of setup and practice. It offers screen reader mode, keyboard-first navigation, the command palette, accessible views for certain panels, and a lot of configurability. For many blind users, it becomes a very capable environment once they learn the patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And low vision?

speaker: ALEX
text: Also strong. VS Code supports themes, high contrast themes, zoom controls, font scaling, custom editor fonts, line height adjustments, minimap controls, panel placement, and many layout preferences. It's one of the more adaptable environments for low vision users because you can tailor so much of the interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the practical learning-card takeaways for VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think in terms of a few core anchors: the Explorer for files, the editor for content, Source Control for Git changes, the integrated terminal for commands, and the command palette as the universal launcher. Once those are familiar, the environment gets much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So VS Code is often the next step after learning the basics in the browser.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It's commonly the day two tool because it opens the door to fuller development work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next is GitHub Desktop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is for people who want Git without the command line, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very often, yes. GitHub Desktop is a local application focused on Git workflows with a visual interface. It's especially useful for cloning repositories, creating branches, committing changes, viewing diffs, and syncing with GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So what can it do well?

speaker: ALEX
text: It does a nice job with branch management, committing, pushing, pulling, and keeping local and remote work in sync. It can help learners understand the flow of changes without requiring terminal commands. For many people, it lowers the barrier to using Git locally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what can't it do?

speaker: ALEX
text: It is not a full code editor. You'll usually pair it with another editor to actually work on files. It also isn't the best tool for every advanced Git scenario, heavy automation, or terminal-based workflows. Its strength is visual Git operations, not being an all-in-one development environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How about accessibility for screen reader users?

speaker: ALEX
text: The experience can be usable, but it's often more mixed than GitHub dot com or the CLI. Some learners appreciate the simpler conceptual model, while others find certain controls or layouts less efficient with a screen reader than a well-structured web page or terminal output. That means it can be a good fit for some users, but not automatically the best fit for every blind learner.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when should someone choose GitHub Desktop?

speaker: ALEX
text: Choose it when you want local Git workflows with less typing, especially if branching, reviewing diffs, committing, and syncing are your main needs. It's often attractive to learners who want a visual bridge between browser-only work and more advanced local tools.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now for path five: GitHub CLI.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The command-line option can feel intimidating at first.

speaker: ALEX
text: It can, so the getting-started principles matter. Learn it as a small set of repeatable actions, not as a giant wall of commands. Start with just a few goals: authenticate, view repository info, inspect issues or pull requests, and create or manage common tasks. Build confidence through patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is the tool called G H.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. GitHub CLI is the command-line tool named gh. It requires installation. Once installed, it lets you interact with GitHub from the terminal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What can you do there?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can inspect repositories, issues, and pull requests. You can create issues and pull requests. You can check out PRs, review them, merge them, and automate repetitive tasks. It's especially good for scripting, keyboard-driven workflows, and integrating GitHub actions into a broader terminal environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what can't it do?

speaker: ALEX
text: It is not a visual code editor. It won't replace the experience of browsing a site visually or editing files in a full GUI editor. For people who want discoverability through menus and visible controls, the CLI can feel less approachable at first. Some tasks may also still be easier in the browser, depending on the situation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about screen reader experience? I know terminal tools can actually be very efficient.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that is a major point here. For many screen reader users, the CLI can be excellent because terminal output is linear, keyboard-driven, and avoids some of the complexity of dense graphical layouts. GitHub even has accessibility guidance specifically for the CLI. That said, the user still needs comfort with terminal navigation and command syntax.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should someone choose GitHub CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Choose it if you already like the terminal, want automation, or want a highly efficient non-visual workflow. It's often ideal for power users and can also become a favorite accessibility path once the basics click.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Any learning-card mindset for the CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: keep a tiny starter set of commands, learn what each one returns, and reuse them often. The goal is not memorizing everything. It's building a dependable toolkit.

speaker: ALEX
text: So with all five paths on the table, the obvious question is: where should a learner start?

speaker: JAMIE
text: Please say, not with all five at once.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely not with all five at once. For day one, the recommendation is usually GitHub dot com. It requires no installation, it exposes core GitHub concepts clearly, and it's enough for browsing repositories, understanding issues and pull requests, and making simple edits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And day two?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually VS Code, if local setup is ready. That's when learners begin doing fuller editing, using Source Control, maybe trying the terminal, and getting comfortable with a local development workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where does github dot dev fit in that progression?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's a great bridge. If you're not ready to install software but you need more than single-file browser edits, github dot dev is the natural next step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Desktop or the CLI are more preference-based.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If visual Git controls make sense to you, try GitHub Desktop. If the terminal feels natural or appealing, explore GitHub CLI. Switching paths is normal. In fact, it's expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels worth repeating: changing tools is not failure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not at all. It's good workflow design. You might review a pull request in the browser, edit files in VS Code, and use the CLI to check CI status. Or you might stay almost entirely in one tool. Both are fine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card here is basically: start simple, add tools when they solve a real problem, and expect your path to evolve.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly put.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's close with a confidence exercise. The goal is to give every learner one small success in their chosen path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the task?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open a repository and make a tiny, safe change workflow-wise. Depending on permissions and the workshop setup, that might mean navigating to a file, opening an editor, making or preparing a trivial edit such as adding a note in a practice file, reviewing the diff, and getting to the point where you could commit the change or understand how you would.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the real goal is not producing important content. It's proving, I can move through the tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Here's how that looks in each path. If you're using GitHub dot com, open the repository in your browser, navigate to a file, use the built-in edit option for a single-file change, review what changed, and look at the commit flow. If you're using github dot dev, open the repository with the period key or the github dot dev URL, make a small edit in the browser editor, inspect the changed files, and explore the commit interface. If you're using VS Code and it's already set up, open the local folder or cloned repository, edit a file, then use Source Control or the terminal to inspect changes and prepare a commit. If you're using GitHub Desktop, open or clone the repository there, edit the file in your preferred editor, return to GitHub Desktop to review the diff, add a commit message, and get ready to commit and sync. If you're using GitHub CLI, navigate to the repository locally, make a small change in your editor of choice, use Git commands and gh where appropriate to inspect status or connect the work back to GitHub, and practice the command-line flow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what does success look like?

speaker: ALEX
text: Success is not perfection. Success is being able to say, I know where the file is, I know where edits happen in this tool, I know where changes show up, and I know what the next step toward a commit would be.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a very learner-friendly definition.

speaker: ALEX
text: It needs to be. Early confidence matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, don't interpret that as choosing the wrong path. Getting stuck is normal when learning GitHub. Here's the practical recovery plan: switch to the simplest path available, usually GitHub dot com, and confirm the underlying task. Read the prompt again and identify the action in plain language: open a file, edit text, review changes, make a commit, open a pull request. Then ask, where does this action live in my chosen tool? If a tool feels too heavy right now, step back rather than pushing through confusion. You can postpone VS Code, Desktop, or CLI and still make meaningful progress in the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So reduce complexity first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And use authoritative documentation when needed. The course points learners to official VS Code setup docs, GitHub Desktop docs, the GitHub CLI manual, and GitHub's accessibility guidance for the CLI. There are also appendices dedicated to VS Code, GitHub Desktop, GitHub CLI, and cloud editors like Codespaces.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's helpful because it tells learners where to go when they need a trustworthy reference, not just a random tutorial.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And one more administrative point: throughout the workshop, later steps include tool cards that show how to do the same task in different environments. So you are not expected to remember every path from this one lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: You just need enough of a map to recognize your options.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the goal. For now, if you can answer these questions, you're in great shape: Which tool needs no installation? Which one gives you a richer browser editor? Which one is the full local development environment? Which one is the visual Git app? Which one is the command-line path? And which of those feels like the easiest place for you to begin?

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the best answer might simply be: today, I'll start in the browser.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is an excellent answer.
